chapters.title: Past Tense — Perfekt
chapters.description: Talk about past events using the Perfekt tense.

lessons.prompt: What is the Perfekt of 'essen' (to eat) for 'ich'?
lessons.explanation: Perfekt = haben/sein + past participle. 'Gegessen' is the past participle of 'essen'.

lessons.prompt: Er ___ nach Berlin gefahren. (He drove to Berlin.)
lessons.explanation: Movement verbs use 'sein' in Perfekt. 'Fahren' → 'ist gefahren'.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'I have already eaten'
What is the past participle of 'schreiben' (to write)?
lessons.explanation: Strong verbs change their vowel: schreiben → geschrieben. ge- + stem + -en.

lessons.prompt: Wir haben das Buch ___. (We have read the book.)
lessons.explanation: 'Lesen' → 'gelesen'. Strong verb with vowel change: e → e (but internal vowel changes: lesen → gelesen).

learningMaterial.title: Past Tense — Perfekt
learningMaterial.intro: Talk about past events using the Perfekt tense.

concepts.explanation: Perfekt = haben/sein + past participle. 'Gegessen' is the past participle of 'essen'.
Movement verbs use 'sein' in Perfekt. 'Fahren' → 'ist gefahren'.
'Schon' = already. 'Gegessen' = past participle of essen.
Strong verbs change their vowel: schreiben → geschrieben. ge- + stem + -en.
'Lesen' → 'gelesen'. Strong verb with vowel change: e → e (but internal vowel changes: lesen → gelesen).

chapters.title: Modal Verbs
chapters.description: Use können, müssen, wollen, dürfen, sollen and mögen correctly.

lessons.prompt: What does 'Ich muss arbeiten' mean?
lessons.explanation: 'Müssen' = must/have to. It expresses necessity or obligation.

lessons.prompt: Er ___ sehr gut Deutsch sprechen. (He can speak German very well.)
lessons.explanation: 'Können' = can/to be able to. Conjugation: ich kann, du kannst, er/sie/es kann.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'You may not smoke here'
lessons.hint: 'Dürfen' = may/to be allowed. Negated = not allowed. 'Rauchen' = to smoke.

lessons.prompt: Which modal verb expresses a wish or desire?
lessons.explanation: 'Wollen' = to want (strong desire/intention). 'Möchten' is softer: would like.

lessons.prompt: Du ___ mehr Wasser trinken. (You should drink more water.)
lessons.explanation: 'Sollen' = should/supposed to (advice or instruction from someone else).

learningMaterial.title: Modal Verbs
learningMaterial.intro: Use können, müssen, wollen, dürfen, sollen and mögen correctly.

concepts.explanation: 'Müssen' = must/have to. It expresses necessity or obligation.
'Können' = can/to be able to. Conjugation: ich kann, du kannst, er/sie/es kann.
'Dürfen' = may/to be allowed. Negated = not allowed. 'Rauchen' = to smoke.
'Wollen' = to want (strong desire/intention). 'Möchten' is softer: would like.
'Sollen' = should/supposed to (advice or instruction from someone else).

chapters.title: Adjective Endings
chapters.description: Master the tricky adjective declension system in German.

lessons.prompt: Complete: 'Der ___ Mann' (the old man — nominative masculine)
lessons.explanation: After definite article (der/die/das) in nominative: adjective + -e for masc/fem/neut.

lessons.prompt: Ich sehe den alt___ Mann. (I see the old man — accusative)
lessons.explanation: After definite article in accusative masculine: adjective + -en. 'Den alten Mann'.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'A cold winter' (nominative, neuter)
lessons.hint: After 'ein' (indefinite article) in nominative neuter, adjective takes strong ending -er... wait, 'Winter' is masculine! Ein kalter Winter.

lessons.prompt: What ending does an adjective take after 'keine' in accusative feminine?
lessons.explanation: After kein/keine in accusative: feminine = -e. 'Keine gute Idee' = no good idea.

lessons.prompt: Mit dem neu___ Auto fährt er gern. (He likes driving the new car.)
lessons.explanation: 'Mit' takes dative. After 'dem' in dative, all genders: adjective + -en.

learningMaterial.title: Adjective Endings
learningMaterial.intro: Master the tricky adjective declension system in German.

concepts.explanation: After definite article (der/die/das) in nominative: adjective + -e for masc/fem/neut.
After definite article in accusative masculine: adjective + -en. 'Den alten Mann'.
After 'ein' (indefinite article) in nominative neuter, adjective takes strong ending -er... wait, 'Winter' is masculine! Ein kalter Winter.
After kein/keine in accusative: feminine = -e. 'Keine gute Idee' = no good idea.
'Mit' takes dative. After 'dem' in dative, all genders: adjective + -en.

chapters.title: Separable & Inseparable Verbs
chapters.description: Understand how prefix verbs work in German sentences.

lessons.prompt: Where does the prefix go in 'aufmachen' (to open) in a main clause?
lessons.explanation: Separable verbs split: 'Ich mache die Tür auf.' (I open the door.) Prefix goes to end.

lessons.prompt: Ich rufe dich morgen ___. (I'll call you back tomorrow.)
lessons.explanation: 'Anrufen' = to call (on phone). Separable: 'Ich rufe... an'. 'An' goes to sentence end.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'The shop opens at nine'
lessons.correctAnswer: Das Geschäft macht um neun auf
lessons.hint: 'Aufmachen' = to open. Separable verb: 'macht...auf'. 'Um neun' = at nine.

lessons.prompt: Which prefix makes a verb INSEPARABLE?
lessons.explanation: Inseparable prefixes (never split): be-, ge-, er-, ver-, zer-, ent-, emp-, miss-.

lessons.prompt: Er ___ das Buch nicht. (He doesn't understand the book.)
lessons.explanation: 'Verstehen' = to understand. Inseparable verb (ver-): stays together. Er versteht.

learningMaterial.title: Separable & Inseparable Verbs
learningMaterial.intro: Understand how prefix verbs work in German sentences.

concepts.explanation: Separable verbs split: 'Ich mache die Tür auf.' (I open the door.) Prefix goes to end.
'Anrufen' = to call (on phone). Separable: 'Ich rufe... an'. 'An' goes to sentence end.

concepts.term: Das Geschäft macht um neun auf
concepts.explanation: 'Aufmachen' = to open. Separable verb: 'macht...auf'. 'Um neun' = at nine.
Inseparable prefixes (never split): be-, ge-, er-, ver-, zer-, ent-, emp-, miss-.
'Verstehen' = to understand. Inseparable verb (ver-): stays together. Er versteht.

chapters.title: Subordinate Clauses
chapters.description: Build complex sentences using conjunctions and verb-final word order.

lessons.prompt: What happens to the verb in a 'weil' (because) clause?
lessons.explanation: Subordinating conjunctions push the verb to the END of the clause. 'Ich bleibe zuhause, weil ich krank bin.'

lessons.prompt: Ich weiß, dass er morgen ___ kommt. (I know that he is coming tomorrow.)
lessons.explanation: In 'dass' clauses, verb goes last. Word order: dass + subject + adverbs + verb. 'Dass er morgen nicht kommt'.

lessons.prompt: Translate: 'She studies because she wants to become a doctor'
lessons.correctAnswer: Sie studiert weil sie Ärztin werden will
lessons.hint: After 'weil', the modal 'will' goes to the very end.

lessons.prompt: Which conjunction means 'although'?
lessons.explanation: 'Obwohl' = although/even though. Also pushes verb to end: 'Obwohl es regnet, gehe ich spazieren.'

lessons.prompt: Er fragt, ___ der Zug pünktlich ist. (He asks whether the train is on time.)
lessons.explanation: 'Ob' = whether/if (in indirect questions). Verb goes to end: 'ob der Zug pünktlich ist'.

learningMaterial.title: Subordinate Clauses
learningMaterial.intro: Build complex sentences using conjunctions and verb-final word order.

concepts.explanation: Subordinating conjunctions push the verb to the END of the clause. 'Ich bleibe zuhause, weil ich krank bin.'
In 'dass' clauses, verb goes last. Word order: dass + subject + adverbs + verb. 'Dass er morgen nicht kommt'.

concepts.term: Sie studiert weil sie Ärztin werden will
concepts.explanation: After 'weil', the modal 'will' goes to the very end.
'Obwohl' = although/even though. Also pushes verb to end: 'Obwohl es regnet, gehe ich spazieren.'
'Ob' = whether/if (in indirect questions). Verb goes to end: 'ob der Zug pünktlich ist'.
